Client 2: And I’m Mary. Same—also just… reassurance that we’re okay and not taking on more risk than we need.
Advisor: Totally. Let’s start high level and then we’ll drill in. Thoma, what do you have as your household gross income per year—before taxes?
Client 1: Household gross is 148400 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that’s combining both of your work income streams as they hit over the year.
Advisor: Next bucket: spending. Mary, about what do your total monthly expenses run—mortgage or rent, groceries, childcare if any, everything?

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And that’s combining both of your work income streams as they hit over the year.

Client 2: We track it pretty loosely, but it’s 6000 a month—like, total monthly expenses are 6000.
Advisor: Okay, I’m noting $6,000 a month. Now on the debt side—any monthly debt payments? Credit cards, student loans, car loans—anything like that?
Client 1: No, monthly debt payments are 0. We don’t have loans outstanding right now.

Client 2: And I’m Mary. Same—also just… reassurance that we’re okay and not taking on more risk than we need.

Advisor: Great—clean slate on debt payments.
Advisor: Let’s talk investments and cash next. When you add up the accounts you consider investable—brokerage, retirement accounts, cash you’d deploy—what’s the total you’re working with?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 342700.
Advisor: Thanks. We’ll break that into the actual accounts in a minute so we’re not relying on one big number.
Advisor: Just so I have the full picture: you also own a home, right? We’ll treat that separately from investable assets.

Advisor: Okay—thanks. Since you mentioned the home, let’s pin down the property and any loans tied to it, then we’ll circle back to the accounts.
Advisor: Mary, what do you have as the current value of the home—just your best estimate is fine.
Client 2: We have it at 255750.
Advisor: Got it. And is that based on a recent appraisal, or more like a Zestimate-type estimate?
Client 2: More like an online estimate—we haven’t had an appraisal lately.

Client 2: And I’m Mary. Same—also just… reassurance that we’re okay and not taking on more risk than we need.

Advisor: No worries. And then just to keep the buckets clean: the home we already captured separately at 255750, and you said there’s no mortgage. So investable is those accounts, home is separate. Cool.
Client 1: Yep.
Advisor: Great. Now, zooming out a bit—when markets get choppy, how would you describe your comfort level? Are you more aggressive, moderate, or conservative?
Client 2: Conservative. I really don’t like big swings.
Client 1: Same—conservative. I’d rather not take a big drawdown.

Advisor: Hi Thoma, hi Mary—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Thoma. A win is just getting everything organized—income, spending, investments—and making sure we’re not missing something obvious.
Client 2: And I’m Mary. Same—also just… reassurance that we’re okay and not taking on more risk than we need.
Advisor: Totally. Let’s start high level and then we’ll drill in. Thoma, what do you have as your household gross income per year—before taxes?

Advisor: Okay, conservative—makes sense. Next one is just a planning detail: you’re based in the US, right?
Client 2: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. And in terms of how we’d work together—are you looking for an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship, versus like a brokerage-only setup?
Client 1: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Got it. Last planning assumption: what tax bracket are you generally landing in these days—do you know the band?

Client 2: I think it’s like… thirty-something percent? I’m not sure.
Advisor: That’s totally fine—Thoma, do you know what you’ve been using?
Client 1: We’ve been using 35%.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. And just to close the loop on the categories we’ve covered today: income we already captured, assets include the brokerage, retirement, bank cash, Mary’s private markets, plus the home; liabilities are zero; and protection policies are none right now. We’ll talk next steps after this.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. Before we wrap today, I want to lock in a couple household basics for the plan. First, marital status—are you two married?

Advisor: Totally fine. Now, on the mortgage—do you have a mortgage balance outstanding right now, or is it paid off?
Client 1: Mortgage balance outstanding is 0. It’s paid off.
Advisor: Nice. Any other loans at all—like a HELOC, car loan, student loans—anything that would show up as loans outstanding?
Client 1: Loans outstanding total is 0.
Advisor: Okay. And just to be thorough, any non-mortgage debt outstanding—credit cards you carry month to month, personal loans, anything like that?

Client 1: We’ve been using 35%.

Advisor: Great. And on protection, you said earlier nothing formal—so no life insurance policies and no disability policies in place right now, correct?

Advisor: Got it. And in the household, you’re the primary person we’re anchoring the plan around—does “primary” fit as your role?
Client 1: Yes—primary.
Advisor: Okay. And what would you call your general job family—like operations, finance, tech, sales—what bucket fits best?
Client 1: Operations.
Advisor: Perfect. Last one on you, Thoma—what’s your gross annual income these days? Ballpark is fine, but if you know the exact number, even better.

Advisor: Actually—before we hop off, Thoma, Mary, I realized I’m missing a couple basics about work. Mind if I grab those quickly?
Client 1: Sure, go ahead.
Advisor: Thoma, first: are you currently employed, or self-employed, or something else?
Client 1: Employed.
Advisor: Got it. And in the household, you’re the primary person we’re anchoring the plan around—does “primary” fit as your role?
